topic: Ramadan schedule — fasting and working long hours

turns.content: how do people survive Ramadan and a full time job at the same time because I am STRUGGLING rn
Ramadan with a full work schedule is genuinely tough, especially the first week while your body adjusts. What's getting to you most — the hunger, the tiredness, or the focus issues?

honestly the focus. I can handle not eating during the day because I've done this my whole life but the dehydration hits different when you're trying to think clearly. like I was staring at a section drawing today for 20 minutes and I swear my brain was just static. no thoughts just vibes and not the good kind

The dehydration-focus connection is real — even mild dehydration (1-2% body water loss) measurably reduces cognitive performance, especially spatial reasoning and attention to detail. Both of which are pretty critical for section drawings.

A few strategies that might help:

1. **Front-load your hydration**: Between iftar and suhoor, aim for 2-3 liters of water. Sip consistently rather than chugging
2. **Shift your hard thinking**: If possible, do your most demanding work in the first 3-4 hours of the day when hydration levels are still decent from suhoor
3. **Afternoon = admin mode**: Save emails, organizing files, non-critical tasks for the afternoon brain-fog window
4. **Electrolytes at suhoor**: A pinch of salt in your water or an electrolyte tablet makes a huge difference for retention

Does your office adjust working hours during Ramadan?

yeah we do reduced hours thankfully, it's UAE law during Ramadan so we do 9-3 instead of 9-6. but tbh the workload doesn't reduce with the hours if you know what I mean lol. we have a lot going on at the office rn so I'm still bringing work home after iftar

The classic "shorter hours, same deadlines" problem. That's frustrating because the reduced hours are supposed to acknowledge that fasting affects your capacity, but if the work just follows you home then you're basically working the same amount with less fuel.

If you're working after iftar, at least you have the advantage of being fed and hydrated. Some people find they get a really productive window from about 9-11 PM after they've eaten and prayed and the food has settled. Your body gets a second wind.

A schedule that might work:

- **Suhoor**: Hydrate aggressively, complex carbs, protein
- **9 AM - 12 PM**: Deep focus work (drawings, design decisions)
- **12 - 3 PM**: Light tasks, meetings, coordination
- **Iftar + rest**: Eat, pray, decompress
- **9 - 11 PM**: Second productive block if needed

The key is accepting that you're operating at maybe 70% capacity during fasting hours and not beating yourself up about it. Does that feel realistic with your current deadlines?

the 70% thing is hard to accept when your brain is telling you that everyone else is managing fine and you're the only one who can't function. but then I look around the office and everyone looks equally zombified by 2pm so maybe we're all just pretending lol

You're definitely all pretending. That's the unspoken Ramadan office solidarity — everyone is running on fumes and mildly dizzy but maintaining professional composure. I guarantee the person sitting across from you who looks like they're doing great is also staring at their screen seeing static.

And think about it — your brain is literally running on stored glycogen and ketones instead of a steady glucose supply. It's not a willpower issue, it's biochemistry. You wouldn't expect a car to perform the same on half a tank with no refueling stops.

The fact that you're aware of the dip in focus and actively strategizing around it is already more than most people do. A lot of people just push through and produce subpar work without realizing it. You're being honest about your limits, which is smarter.

I mean when you put it like that... ok fine I'll stop being so hard on myself. also can we talk about suhoor for a sec because I think mine is terrible. I've been eating whatever leftovers from iftar are in the fridge which is usually rice and something heavy and then I feel like a brick for the first 2 hours of work

Yeah, leftover rice at suhoor is filling you up fast but then spiking your blood sugar and crashing it. That heavy bloated feeling in the morning is your body diverting energy to digest a dense meal right before you need to be alert.

Better suhoor strategy:

- **Oats with banana and peanut butter** — slow-release carbs, potassium, sustained energy
- **Eggs** (any style) — protein keeps you satiated longer than rice
- **Labneh or Greek yogurt with honey** — easy on the stomach, good protein
- **Dates + nuts** — the classic for a reason
- **Avocado on toast** — healthy fats slow digestion in a good way

And critically: **water, water, water**. Drink a full glass before eating, sip throughout, and finish with another glass. Add a pinch of Himalayan salt or an electrolyte sachet to one of them.

The goal at suhoor is slow-burn fuel, not volume. You want to feel comfortable, not stuffed.

oats and banana sounds so much better than cold machboos at 4am wallah. I'm gonna meal prep some overnight oats this weekend so they're just ready to grab. also the electrolyte thing — my trainer has been telling me the same thing and I keep forgetting to buy the sachets

Overnight oats are perfect for suhoor because there's zero effort at 4 AM — you literally just open the fridge and eat. Make 3-4 jars on Friday and you're set. You can do different toppings: one with banana and peanut butter, one with dates and almonds, one with berries and honey. Variety keeps you from getting bored of it by week two.

And go buy the electrolyte sachets today! Set a reminder right now. Your trainer and I are in agreement so you have no excuses. Brands like O.R.S or even the pharmacy rehydration salts work fine — you don't need anything fancy.

Are you still training during Ramadan or taking a break?

I'm training but lighter and only after iftar. no way I'm doing anything intense while fasting that would actually end me. ok yalla I need to go prep for tomorrow, early morning. thanks for the suhoor advice especially I think that's gonna make a huge difference. Ramadan kareem!!

Ramadan kareem! Training after iftar at a lighter intensity is exactly right — listen to your body this month. And yes, fix the suhoor situation first, it'll change your entire daytime experience.

Go prep those overnight oats this weekend and buy those electrolyte sachets. Your 2 PM self will thank you. Hope the rest of the month gets smoother!
